thread.title: Palm Bay City Council fires city manager, in 3-2 vote, with support of two new members
thread.title_full: Palm Bay City Council fires city manager, in 3-2 vote, with support of two new members

author: , Florida Today
title: Palm Bay City Council fires city manager, in 3-2 vote, with support of two new members
text: The Palm Bay City Council voted 3-2 on Thursday night to fire City Manager Suzanne Sherman, with two of the three newly elected council members supporting the firing. The item was not on the council agenda. But one of the new council members, Chandler Langevin, brought the matter up during council reports at the end of the meeting, which was the first meeting for the new councilors following a shakeup of the City Council in the Nov. 5 election . Joining Langevin in voting to get rid of Sherman were Councilmen Mike Hammer and Kenny Johnson. Voting to keep Sherman were Mayor Rob Medina and Deputy Mayor Mike Jaffe. Hammer and Jaffe are the other two new council members. "I led the charge to relieve the city manager of Palm Bay," Langevin said, in explaining his action. "This new council was given a mandate by the voters to enact sweeping changes to reverse the course of our city’s decline. The city historically has lacked any vision or plans to accommodate our massive growth, has hemorrhaged taxpayer dollars on waste and abuse, and has stagnated our local economy." Langevin said Palm Bay "is in a uniquely poor state that will take strong leadership to climb out of. My vision calls for a proven conservative leader with heavy experience in both private executive leadership and city management. I need a manager that is capable of drastically reducing our budget, while increasing services. This can be done through implementing efficiency and by working with developers and community partners to contribute to the city. We don’t have an easy task ahead of us, but it’s a task worth accomplishing for all the residents of Palm Bay." Hammer said he was not expecting the issue to come up on Thursday. But when Langevin introduced the resolution, Hammer supported it, based on Hammer's campaign promise to bring new leadership into city government. Hammer said Sherman is "an amazing person," but he did not believe she was the best person to tackle the issues facing Palm Bay, which is Brevard County's most populous city, with a population of more than 140,000. "We need somebody who is better equipped for the job," Hammer said. Hammer said he didn't want to get into details. But he said he stood by his earlier comments on his campaign questionnaire to FLORIDA TODAY, in which Hammer wrote: "Our current operational structure is inefficient, with unqualified leaders, uncontrolled development, and a reliance on consultants, instead of our own talented engineers. "Our road program is mismanaged, not just monetarily, but prioritizing less critical projects over vital ones. Attracting new businesses is crucial to diversify our economy and reduce the tax burden on residents. We need a strategic plan with targeted incentives and a streamlined permitting processes. "Finally, we need to prioritize self-sufficiency by a stronger infrastructure; less multifamily developments; more smart business; and reallocating impact fees for police, fire and infrastructure projects." Hammer said many of the other City Council candidates in the 2024 election also indicated that they wanted to see a change in direction in city leadership. Langevin's resolution was straightforward, saying: "The City Council hereby removes Suzanne Sherman as city manager by terminating her employment with the city of Palm Bay, without cause. This resolution shall be effective as of Nov. 21, 2024." Sherman had been Palm Bay city manager since January 2021, and before that served as interim city manager since July 2020. She worked from 2006 to 2015 in Palm Bay's human resources and utilities departments. She was serving as acting deputy city manager/assistant to the city manager when she resigned and headed to Satellite Beach City Hall. There, she worked as assistant city manager and support services department director. Sherman returned to Palm Bay in January 2019 to become deputy city manager. In July 2020, council members named her acting city manager after former City Manager Lisa Morrell resigned. There have been rumors that the City Council could recruit former Palm Bay City Manager Greg Lynk to return to that position. But Hammer said he would like to have a national search done for a new city manager, rather than rehire a former city manager. The City Council has scheduled a special workshop for 6 p.m. Monday at City Hall to discuss options for hiring a city manager. In the meantime, the City Council named Information Technologies Director Brian Robinson to serve as interim city manager. Hammer said getting rid of the city manager "was not something I wanted to do on Day 1" of his being a City Council member. But when Langevin's resolution was introduced, he felt he should support it, in alignment with his own campaign platforms. Dave Berman is business editor at FLORIDA TODAY. Contact Berman at [dberman@floridatoday.com](mailto:dberman@floridatoday.com), on X at @bydaveberman and on Facebook at www.facebook.com/dave.berman.54
